questions.question_text: A California spouse uses her salary to purchase a rental property during marriage, taking title in her name only. Her husband claims an interest. Who is correct?
questions.correct_answer: Husband has a community interest because marital earnings are community property
distractors: Wife has sole ownership because title is in her name only
Husband has an interest only if he contributed to the purchase
Neither spouse owns it — marital property requires joint title
questions.explanation: California is a community property state. Property acquired with earnings during marriage is community property regardless of whose name is on title. Each spouse owns an equal one-half interest by operation of law.

questions.question_text: A married California seller wants to sell a community property home. Her husband refuses to sign the listing or purchase agreement. Can she sell alone?
questions.correct_answer: No, both spouses must consent to sell or encumber community real property
distractors: Yes, either spouse can convey community property unilaterally
Yes, but only if she was the primary earner who bought the property
No, but she can list it and her husband can object after an offer is received
questions.explanation: Both spouses must consent in writing to sell, convey, or encumber community real property in California. One spouse cannot unilaterally transfer or mortgage community real property without the other's joinder.

questions.question_text: A California spouse inherits a beach house from her parents during her marriage. Her husband claims half ownership. Which rule applies?
questions.correct_answer: The inheritance is separate property and the husband has no claim
distractors: The inheritance is community property because it was received during marriage
The house is separate property unless the wife adds him to title
The house is community property because real estate is always community
questions.explanation: Property received as a gift or inheritance during marriage is separate property in California, not community property. The spouse who received the inheritance retains sole ownership. The other spouse has no community property claim.

questions.question_text: A California couple wants to convert the husband's separate property house into community property. What is required under California transmutation law?
questions.correct_answer: A written document with an express declaration of transmutation
distractors: A verbal agreement between both spouses is sufficient
Recording a new deed with both names satisfies the requirement
A court order confirming the change of character
questions.explanation: California requires transmutation of real property to be in writing with an express declaration that the character of the property is being changed. Simply adding a name to a deed is insufficient — the document must explicitly state the intent to transmute.

questions.question_text: A California couple divorces. Their home was purchased during marriage with community funds. How is the property divided?
questions.correct_answer: The property is divided 50/50 as community property
distractors: The court decides based on each spouse's contribution
The spouse who holds title keeps the property
The property must be sold and proceeds split based on income
questions.explanation: California is a community property state and follows equal division upon divorce. Community property is divided equally (50/50) between spouses, regardless of which spouse earned more or whose name appears on title.

questions.question_text: Three siblings inherit a California property in equal shares. They did not specify the form of ownership. Which form of co-ownership applies by default?
distractors: Joint tenancy with right of survivorship
Community property
Tenancy by the entirety
questions.explanation: When property is co-owned without specifying the form, California law defaults to tenancy in common. Joint tenancy requires specific language in the deed (including 'as joint tenants'). Tenancy by the entirety does not exist in California.

questions.question_text: A California co-owner holds property as a joint tenant. She dies without a will. What happens to her interest?
questions.correct_answer: Her interest automatically passes to the surviving joint tenant(s)
distractors: Her interest passes to her children through intestate succession
Her estate receives her share and can sell it
The property must be sold and proceeds distributed to all heirs
questions.explanation: Joint tenancy includes the right of survivorship. When a joint tenant dies, her interest automatically passes to the surviving joint tenant(s) by operation of law — bypassing probate and any will or intestate succession.

questions.question_text: A California couple holds title as 'community property with right of survivorship.' What advantage does this form of title provide over standard community property?
questions.correct_answer: It avoids probate while preserving the full stepped-up tax basis
distractors: It allows unilateral sale without the other spouse's consent
It provides creditor protection not available under community property
It converts community property into separate property at death
questions.explanation: Community property with right of survivorship avoids probate (like joint tenancy) while preserving the full double stepped-up basis at the first spouse's death (like community property). Standard joint tenancy only provides a step-up on the deceased's half.

questions.question_text: A California homeowner creates a revocable living trust and transfers title into the trust. Why do California estate planning attorneys frequently recommend this strategy?
questions.correct_answer: Trust-held property avoids probate at the owner's death
distractors: Trusts eliminate property taxes and transfer taxes
Trusts provide blanket protection from all creditors
Trusts allow future heirs to avoid capital gains tax entirely
questions.explanation: Living trusts are extremely common in California because property held in trust at death passes directly to beneficiaries without probate — a significant benefit given California's lengthy and costly probate process. The trust does not eliminate property taxes or provide automatic creditor protection.

questions.question_text: To create a valid joint tenancy in California, which set of requirements must be met at the time of creation?
questions.correct_answer: Time, title, interest, and possession — the four unities
distractors: Spouses only, equal shares, joint occupancy, and one deed
Written agreement, equal shares, and survivorship language
Notarized deed, equal shares, and recorded with county
questions.explanation: Joint tenancy requires the four unities (TTIP): Time (acquired at the same time), Title (same deed or instrument), Interest (equal shares), and Possession (equal right of possession). The deed must also include specific joint tenancy language.

questions.question_text: Under California's homestead exemption, how is the amount of protected equity determined for a homeowner?
questions.correct_answer: The greater of a statutory minimum or countywide median, up to a CPI-adjusted cap
distractors: A flat $300,000 for all homeowners statewide
The full market value of the home regardless of equity
The amount set by the local county assessor each year
questions.explanation: California's homestead exemption (CCP §704.730, updated by AB 1885) protects equity equal to the greater of a statutory minimum floor or the countywide median sale price for a single-family home, subject to a maximum cap. Both the floor and cap are adjusted annually for CPI, so the specific amounts increase over time.

questions.question_text: A California homeowner has an automatic homestead. A mechanic's lien was recorded before she moved in. Can the homestead exemption protect her from this lien?
questions.correct_answer: No, the homestead does not protect against mechanic's liens recorded before the homestead
distractors: Yes, the homestead protects against all liens once she occupies the property
Yes, but only if the lien is under $300,000
No, and the homestead also does not protect from property tax liens
questions.explanation: California's homestead exemption does not protect against mechanic's liens that attached before the homestead was established, purchase-money mortgages, or tax liens. It only protects against most general judgment creditors after the homestead is established.

questions.question_text: What is the key difference between a declared homestead and an automatic homestead in California?
questions.correct_answer: Declared homestead requires recording a declaration; automatic applies to owner-occupants by statute
distractors: Automatic homestead provides more equity protection than declared
Only married homeowners qualify for the automatic homestead
Declared homestead must be renewed every 5 years to remain effective
questions.explanation: California's automatic homestead (CCP §704.730) applies by statute to any owner who occupies the home as a primary residence without any filing required. A declared homestead requires recording a formal declaration but historically offered different protections. Modern law primarily uses the automatic homestead.

questions.question_text: A California homestead protects a primary residence. Which type of property is NOT eligible for the homestead exemption?
questions.correct_answer: A rental property where the owner does not reside
distractors: A condominium used as the owner's primary residence
A mobile home on owned land used as primary residence
A single-family home in a planned unit development
questions.explanation: The California homestead exemption protects only the owner's primary residence. A rental property where the owner does not reside does not qualify. The exemption requires actual owner occupancy as the principal dwelling.

questions.question_text: A California homeowner has a purchase-money mortgage on her primary residence. She falls behind on payments and claims the homestead exemption to prevent foreclosure. Will the homestead protect her?
questions.correct_answer: No, purchase-money loans are exempt from homestead protection
distractors: Yes, the homestead protects against all foreclosures
Yes, but only up to the statutory minimum amount
No, but she can file a declared homestead to gain protection
questions.explanation: The California homestead exemption does not protect against purchase-money mortgages or trust deeds, property tax liens, or mechanic's liens recorded before the homestead attached. The homestead only protects against general judgment creditors seeking to force a sale of the primary residence.
